title: DREAM MACHINE | Creative AI News & Insight { OCT 25 [2] }

Welcome to the second edition of DREAM MACHINE. News and insight on AI in the Creative Industries: covering Film, Music, Games, Media & Entertainment.
Made for creators, developers, dreamers and content peeps who want to stay on top of things going on, because it's a lot to take in. Created with
Dr John O'Hare
Steve Moyler
& the
dreamlab
community.
Editors Pick
OpenAI released its agents this week, but what could that mean for the Creative Industries?
Agentic AI—the class of AI systems that can plan, act, and pursue goals with autonomy—promises a new era of collaboration in creative industries. Unlike passive “prompt-and-respond” tools, agentic systems can assume roles: a design companion, an editing assistant, a rights manager, a researcher for a newsletter (🧐) or a workflow orchestrator. Its another step along the Human-AI Agency Continuum:
Human-AI Agency Continuum.
In creative workflows, agentic AI can handle routine but essential tasks—asset cataloguing, clip indexing, rough cuts, rote research or metadata tagging—so that potentially human creators stay in the high-imagination zone, the human bit. At the same time, agents can propose variations, monitor aesthetic consistency, and flag ethical or attribution issues. Because creative work thrives on iteration, surprise, and feedback, agentic AI becomes a tool
within
the loop and potentially helps with getting more loops/iterations into the process, which could be a positive thing: it suggests, adapts, and learns, while the human remains the ultimate arbiter of taste, meaning, nuance and cultural value.
That said, the path forward isn’t without tension. Questions around authorship, attribution, fairness, and the balance of control are very real in an agentic AI era. The creative industries must experiment, define best practices, and embed safeguards, consider the implications of a more systems based media world to ensure that agentic AI remains a collaborator—and not a “black box” usurper.
Here's another article about how agents could support the media industries.

Industry Insight / Interesting Stats of the Week
Only 10% of music-makers would listen to AI-generated music, says research from Ditto Music.
Earlier this year Ditto also conducted a survey revealing around
48% of surveyed musicians have used AI in their music
which is down from their 2023 survey!
47% of Brits claim AI is saving them up to four hours per week on home tasks and life admin
